question: Can I keep a hash of my Stellar secret key on-chain so I can get it back later?

golden.answer: No. A cryptographic hash is one-way: it is not a backup from which the original secret key can be recovered. Encrypted ciphertext is different: it is recoverable only with the separate decryption key or password and the correct scheme; putting ciphertext on-chain makes it public and permanent, so weak-password and offline-guessing risk remains. Publishing a raw secret key on-chain is unsafe, and a database or password manager only helps if it stores a properly protected backup that you can actually recover; it does not create a network-level reset option. Use the wallet's documented recovery/backup design, keep recovery material private, and test your backup process before relying on it. Do not send a secret key or its recovery phrase to anyone offering recovery help.
keyFacts: States that a hash cannot be reversed to recover the secret key.
avoid: Do NOT say an on-chain key hash enables recovery.
Do NOT advise publishing or sharing a secret key.
golden.notes: Edge cant-do case; no unsafe implementation instructions.

corroboration.claim: Stellar secret keys provide signing authority while public keys identify accounts; publishing raw secret material is unsafe.
A one-way hash is not recoverable ciphertext; encrypted storage requires separate key management and resists recovery only according to its scheme and password strength.

verified.by: P4 H3 primary+blind (Solo 3415/3419) with Fable voice review
